did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and applied a supervised machine-learning algorithm (specifically using the S-statistic computing language and decision-tree models) to predict multidisciplinary team (MDT) treatment recommendations for basal cell carcinoma (BCC), a skin cancer, based on patient and tumor characteristics.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is labeled as a 'Clinical Studies' article and explicitly states its aim was to 'develop a supervised machine-learning algorithm to predict MDT recommendations'. It presents original data from a study population of 304 patients, describes specific methods (S-statistic, decision-tree models), and reports new results (e.g., 45.1% of nasal BCCs could be reliably predicted). It is not a review of prior literature.

title: Machine-learning algorithm to predict multidisciplinary team treatment recommendations in the management of basal cell carcinoma
brief_summary: This study developed a supervised machine-learning algorithm using the S-statistic computing language and decision-tree models to predict multidisciplinary team (MDT) treatment recommendations for nasal basal cell carcinoma (BCC). The dataset comprised 304 consecutive patients with nasal BCC discussed at a specialized skin cancer MDT between January and December 2015. The model utilized patient and tumor characteristics, such as age, anatomical location, histological subtype, and tumor size, to predict whether patients would be triaged to Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) or conventional therapy. The main evaluation approach involved assessing the algorithm's ability to reliably predict MDT decisions against actual clinical outcomes. The key result was that the algorithm could reliably predict the MDT decision outcome for 45.1% of nasal BCCs, with 37.5% specifically predicted for MMS based on tumor location and age. The authors concluded that this approach could reduce MDT caseload and improve efficiency by allowing teams to focus on more complex cases.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study population consisted of 304 consecutive patients with a diagnosis of nasal basal cell carcinoma (BCC) discussed at a skin cancer-specialised multidisciplinary team (SSMDT). The paper also mentions BCC constitutes ~80% of non-melanoma skin cancers and is the most common human cancer.
Mentioned in the introduction to provide context that BCC constitutes ~80% of all non-melanoma skin cancers, but not the specific target of the experimental analysis.

all_task_types.target: Multidisciplinary team (MDT) treatment recommendation (Mohs micrographic surgery vs. conventional therapy)
all_task_types.details: The paper develops a supervised machine-learning algorithm using decision-tree models (RPART) to classify nasal basal cell carcinoma cases into discrete treatment categories: Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) or conventional therapy (surgical excision or radiotherapy). The model predicts discrete diagnostic/therapeutic labels based on patient and tumor characteristics.

datasets_used.name: Norfolk and Norwich University Hospital Skin Cancer-Specialised Multidisciplinary Team (SSMDT) Cohort
datasets_used.description: A retrospective cohort of consecutive patients with a diagnosis of cutaneous basal cell carcinoma (BCC) located on the external nose, discussed at the SSMDT at Norfolk and Norwich University Hospital NHS Foundation Trust between January 1, 2015, and December 31, 2015. Data were routinely collected from electronic patient notes and electronic theatre records.

use.training: Used to develop the supervised machine-learning algorithm (decision-tree models using RPART) to predict MDT treatment recommendations.
use.validation: Used for cross-validation (plotcp) and pruning (prune) of the decision trees to select complexity parameters and assess performance.
use.testing: The entire cohort was used to evaluate the algorithm's ability to predict MDT decisions against actual clinical outcomes (reliability of prediction).
use.other: Used for univariate analysis of demographic and tumour characteristics to identify significant risk factors.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: ability to match the MDT outcome

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To identify statistically significant associated risk factors for MMS vs conventional therapy and analyze trends in tumour characteristics.
To identify statistically significant associated risk factors for MMS vs conventional therapy, specifically used for replicates of ≤5.
To analyse age differences between groups.
To analyse sex differences and quantify the risk of high-risk phenotype in the MMS group.
To check collinearity among environmental descriptors and remove variables with the highest VIF until remaining VIFs were <2.0.
To select a reasonable complexity parameter for the decision-tree predictive model.
To prune decision trees using the complexity parameter identified during cross-validation.
To report the uncertainty for the relative risk of high-risk phenotype in the MMS group.

hypothesis_testing.test: Chi-squared test (χ²), Fisher's exact test, and Mann-Whitney U test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no association between the demographic/tumor characteristic (e.g., age, anticoagulation use, pacemaker presence, anatomical location, histology) and the treatment modality (Mohs micrographic surgery vs. conventional therapy).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant association between the demographic/tumor characteristic and the treatment modality.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined by p-values < 0.05. Specifically, the study reported significant differences in age (p < 0.0001), anticoagulation use (p < 0.0001), pacemaker presence (p = 0.004), and various tumor characteristics (e.g., high-risk phenotype p = 0.0008, nodular histology p = 0.0176) between the MMS and conventional therapy groups, indicating these factors are associated with treatment decisions.

Norfolk and Norwich University Hospital.metric: Reliability of prediction (percentage of cases where MDT decision could be reliably predicted by the algorithm)

Supervised machine-learning algorithm (S.conventional_therapy_prediction_reliabil: Not explicitly quantified as a separate percentage, but implied to be part of the 45.1% total; specific terminal node weights provided for key predictors

Norfolk and Norwich University Hospital.notes: The algorithm reliably predicted the MDT decision outcome for 45.1% of nasal BCCs. Specifically, 37.5% of patients could be reliably predicted to be triaged to Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) based on tumour location and age. The choice of conventional treatment could be reliably predicted based on patient’s age, tumour phenotype, and lesion size. The remaining 54.1% of the casemix required MDT discussion. The study did not report a separate test/validation split accuracy (e.g., AUC, F1-score) but evaluated success by the algorithm's ability to match the MDT outcome. The predictive accuracy was described as 'consistently achieved at a clinically useful level' and 'highly discriminative'.

architecture_used.main_model: Supervised machine-learning algorithm using decision-tree models (specifically the RPART algorithm for recursive partitioning)

architecture_details.feature_selection: Variables with variance-inflation factor (VIF) >= 2.0 were removed; final model used 6 variables: age, anatomical location, histological diagnosis, tumour size, tumour recurrence, and anticoagulation
architecture_details.model_structure: Decision tree with depth and leaf nodes of 2–3
architecture_details.complexity_parameter: Minimum performance cut-off value <0.15 for conventional surgery and >0.85 for MMS (selected via cross-validation)

training_details.dataset: Retrospective cohort of 304 consecutive patients with nasal basal cell carcinoma from Norfolk and Norwich University Hospital SSMDT (Jan 2015 - Dec 2015)
training_details.target_variable: MDT treatment recommendation (Mohs micrographic surgery vs. conventional therapy)
training_details.hyperparameters: Complexity parameter (cp) selected via cross-validation; VIF threshold <2.0 for feature selection

decision_rules: Key terminal nodes identified: 'Tumour is located in the lower region or multiple regions (complex) + <69 years old' (predicts MMS), 'Tumour is located in the middle or upper region + <63 years old' (predicts MMS), '≥78 years old + tumour size >20 mm' (predicts MMS), '≥78 years old + tumour size is ≤20 mm + subtype is a low-risk phenotype (nodular)' (predicts conventional therapy)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Supervised machine-learning algorithm (decision-tree model using RPART algorithm)
Convolutional neural network (mentioned as baseline/comparison in literature review)
conclusions: The supervised machine-learning algorithm reliably predicted the multidisciplinary team (MDT) decision outcome for 45.1% of nasal basal cell carcinoma (BCC) cases.
Specifically, 37.5% of patients could be reliably predicted to be triaged to Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) based on tumor location and age.
The choice of conventional treatment could be reliably predicted based on the patient’s age, tumor phenotype, and lesion size.

The remaining 54.1% of the nasal BCC casemix required MDT discussion, as these cases involve complex factors (e.g., recurrence, financial costs, cosmetic outcome) that cannot be reliably predicted by the algorithm.
Utilizing this algorithm has the potential to reduce the burden and improve the efficiency of the specialist skin MDT by allowing teams to focus on more complex cases.
This approach may improve patient care, reduce waiting times, and reduce the financial burden associated with MDT meetings.

The study acknowledges limitations including the single-unit setting which may limit external validity, the lack of full recording of all potential confounders, and the need for regular updates to account for changes in referral patterns, treatment options, or local clinical expertise.
Further studies at external centers using prospectively collected datasets from multiple institutions are needed to clarify the clinical utility and generalizability of the model.
important_remarks: The study is limited by its single-center design (Norfolk and Norwich University Hospital), which may restrict external validity and generalizability to other institutions with different referral patterns or clinical expertise.

The algorithm's success was measured by its ability to match MDT outcomes rather than clinical outcomes (e.g., survival or recurrence), as BCC has a good prognosis making traditional survival metrics less relevant.
The dataset did not fully record all potential confounders, such as delays in assessment or the specific presence/absence of certain MDT members, which could influence treatment decisions.

The model relies on retrospective data from 2015; the authors note the algorithm would need regular updates to account for changes in patient referral patterns, treatment options, or local clinical expertise.
Data cannot be shared publicly due to patient confidentiality and lack of informed consent for public sharing, limiting independent verification and external validation.

The algorithm reliably predicted MDT decisions for only 45.1% of cases, meaning 54.1% of the casemix still required complex MDT discussion involving factors like financial costs and cosmetic outcomes that are not captured by the model.

The study acknowledges that while the algorithm can reduce MDT workload, the ultimate treatment decision remains with the patient and their specialist, and the tool is intended to support rather than replace core MDT members.
